agent_output:
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy), and the Reporting Service's direct database reads (creating invisible coupling). The synchronous Notification fan-in, the god endpoint on Customer Service, and HTTP-based background jobs each add operational fragility. Remediation should be sequenced to address data coupling first (highest blast radius), then communication patterns, then API design — prioritizing changes that unblock independent deployability.",

  "architecture": {
    "components": [
      { "name": "Order Service", "responsibility": "Order lifecycle management", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Courier, database read directly by Reporting" },
      { "name": "Courier Service", "responsibility": "Delivery assignment and tracking", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Order Service" },
      { "name": "Notification Service", "responsibility": "Multi-channel notifications", "boundaries": "Structurally sound but operationally fragile — synchronous fan-in from 5 callers" },
      { "name": "Payment Service", "responsibility": "Payment processing and refunds", "boundaries": "Partially violated — refund jobs exposed as REST endpoints" },
      { "name": "Customer Service", "responsibility": "Customer profile and related data", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — god endpoint exposes unbounded aggregate" },
      { "name": "Reporting Service", "responsibility": "Analytics and reporting", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — bypasses Order Service API, reads raw tables directly" }
    ],
    "interfaces": [
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Courier Service", "contract": "Shared PostgreSQL instance (schema-prefix separation only)", "coupling_type": "database coupling (worst form)" },
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Courier Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Payment Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Customer Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal coupling" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Order Service DB", "contract": "Direct SQL reads against Order tables", "coupling_type": "database coupling (read parasitism)" },
      { "from": "Cron scheduler", "to": "Payment Service", "contract": "HTTP GET/POST to /api/v1/jobs/process-refunds", "coupling_type": "inappropriate protocol coupling" },
      { "from": "Any consumer", "to": "Customer Service", "contract": "GET /api/v1/everything — returns full customer aggregate", "coupling_type": "stamp coupling (over-fetching)" }
    ],
    "data_flow": "Order and Courier services share a single PostgreSQL instance with schema prefixes as the only boundary. Reporting reads Order tables directly via SQL. Notification receives synchronous calls from 5 services. Background jobs (e.g., refund processing) are triggered by external cron hitting REST endpoints. Customer Service returns monolithic payloads to all consumers regardless of need.",
    "patterns_used": [
      { "pattern": "Shared Database (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Likely started as a monolith; Order and Courier were split into services but the database was never separated" },
      { "pattern": "Synchronous Fan-In (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Notification was built as a utility service; callers integrated the easy way — direct HTTP" },
      { "pattern": "God Endpoint (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Likely built for a single consumer's convenience, then reused by others who only need subsets" },
      { "pattern": "HTTP-triggered Batch Jobs (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Developers reused the existing REST framework rather than introducing a proper job runner" },
      { "pattern": "Direct Database Reads across service boundaries (anti-pattern)", "rationale": "Reporting needed fast queries; calling the API was seen as too slow or insufficiently flexible" }
    ]
  },

  "domain_model": {
    "bounded_contexts": [
      { "name": "Order Management", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Order", "OrderLine", "OrderStatus"] },
      { "name": "Courier & Delivery", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Courier", "Delivery", "Route"] },
      { "name": "Customer Management", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Customer", "Address", "Preferences"] },
      { "name": "Payment", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Payment", "Refund", "PaymentMethod"] },
      { "name": "Notification", "classification": "generic", "aggregates": ["NotificationTemplate", "NotificationLog"] },
      { "name": "Reporting & Analytics", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Report", "ReportSchedule"] }
    ],
    "context_map": [
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Courier & Delivery", "relationship": "SHOULD BE Customer-Supplier; CURRENTLY Shared Kernel via shared DB (inappropriate)" },
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Reporting & Analytics", "relationship": "SHOULD BE Published Language (events/API); CURRENTLY no boundary (direct DB access)" },
      { "upstream": "All Services", "downstream": "Notification", "relationship": "SHOULD BE Async via events; CURRENTLY synchronous Conformist" },
      { "upstream": "Customer Management", "downstream": "All Consumers", "relationship": "SHOULD BE Open Host Service with multiple endpoints; CURRENTLY single god endpoint" }
    ]
  },

  "trade_offs": [
    {
      "decision": "Split shared database between Order and Courier into separate instances",
      "gains": "Independent deployability, independent schema evolution, failure isolation, clear ownership",
      "sacrifices": "Cross-service queries become harder, eventual consistency required for some operations, migration effort is significant",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Keep shared DB but enforce strict schema-level permissions (half-measure, doesn't solve coupling)", "Use database views as a contract layer (reduces but doesn't eliminate coupling)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace synchronous Notification calls with async event bus",
      "gains": "Callers are no longer blocked by Notification failures, Notification can scale independently, natural retry/DLQ semantics",
      "sacrifices": "Adds infrastructure complexity (message broker), notifications become eventually consistent, debugging is harder",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Fire-and-forget HTTP with circuit breaker (reduces but doesn't eliminate temporal coupling)", "Sidecar pattern to handle notification as infrastructure concern"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace Reporting's direct DB reads with a read replica or CDC-fed data store",
      "gains": "Order Service can evolve its schema freely, Reporting gets a model optimized for queries, no production DB load from reports",
      "sacrifices": "Data is eventually consistent (seconds to minutes of lag), CDC infrastructure adds operational complexity",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Expose a dedicated Reporting API on Order Service (limits query flexibility)", "Database views as stable contract (still couples at DB level)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Decompose /api/v1/everything into purpose-specific endpoints",
      "gains": "Reduced payload sizes, independent cacheability, consumers fetch only what they need, easier to evolve each sub-resource",
      "sacrifices": "Consumers making multiple calls for composite views, possible N+1 if not careful with BFF layer",
      "alternatives_considered": ["GraphQL on Customer Service (powerful but high complexity for a single service)", "Keep god endpoint but add field selection via query params (band-aid)"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Move background jobs from HTTP endpoints to a proper job runner",
      "gains": "Built-in retries, concurrency control, idempotency tracking, no accidental external invocation, observability",
      "sacrifices": "New infrastructure component to operate, team needs to learn job framework",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Keep HTTP endpoints but add authentication and idempotency headers (lipstick on a pig)", "Serverless functions triggered by CloudWatch/Cloud Scheduler (viable if already on cloud)"]
    }
  ],

  "flexibility_points": [
    { "area": "Notification delivery mechanism", "evolution_path": "Once async, can swap email/SMS/push providers, add new channels, implement user preferences — all without touching callers" },
    { "area": "Reporting data pipeline", "evolution_path": "CDC-fed read store opens the door to real-time dashboards, ML pipelines, and data lake integration without touching operational services" },
    { "area": "Customer API surface", "evolution_path": "Decomposed endpoints allow independent versioning, deprecation, and caching strategies per sub-resource" },
    { "area": "Background job infrastructure", "evolution_path": "Proper job runner enables scheduled jobs, event-triggered jobs, job chaining, and priority queues as business needs grow" }
  ],

  "rigidity_points": [
    { "area": "Order-Courier database split", "reason": "Once separated, the contract between these services is locked to APIs/events. This is intentional rigidity — schema changes in one service must not cascade to the other." },
    { "area": "Event schema contracts", "reason": "Once services communicate via events, the event schemas become a contract. Use schema registry and versioning from day one — changing event shapes later is painful." },
    { "area": "Customer sub-resource URL structure", "reason": "Once consumers migrate to /customers/{id}/addresses, /customers/{id}/payment-methods, etc., these paths become stable contracts." }
  ],

  "risks": [
    { "risk": "Shared database split causes data inconsistency during migration — Order and Courier may reference each other's records", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Use a strangler fig approach: introduce an API layer first, dual-write during transition, validate consistency, then cut over. Never big-bang." },
    { "risk": "Reporting Service breaks silently when Order schema changes — no compile-time or API contract to catch it", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Immediate: add integration tests that run Reporting queries against Order schema. Medium-term: move to CDC-fed read replica with explicit schema transformation." },
    { "risk": "Notification Service outage cascades to all 5 upstream services due to synchronous coupling", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Immediate: add circuit breakers and timeouts on all Notification callers. Medium-term: migrate to async events." },
    { "risk": "HTTP-exposed background jobs can be triggered by anyone with network access — no authentication, no idempotency", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Immediate: add authentication and idempotency keys. Medium-term: migrate to internal job runner." },
    { "risk": "God endpoint on Customer Service returns payment methods to consumers that don't need them — PCI/data exposure risk", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Immediate: audit all consumers of /everything, strip sensitive fields based on caller identity. Medium-term: decompose endpoint." },
    { "risk": "Team attempts to fix all 5 anti-patterns simultaneously, causing a months-long destabilization", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Sequence remediation strictly. Ship quick wins (circuit breakers, job auth) in week 1-2. Tackle database split as a focused multi-sprint effort." }
  ],

  "implementation_guidance": {
    "recommended_approach": "Phased remediation in 4 waves, ordered by blast radius and quick-win potential.\n\n**Wave 1 — Immediate stabilization (1-2 weeks):**\n• Add circuit breakers + timeouts on all Notification callers\n• Add auth + idempotency to job endpoints\n• Add integration tests for Reporting→Order DB queries\n• Audit /everything consumers and add field filtering\n\n**Wave 2 — Async Notifications (2-4 weeks):**\n• Introduce message broker (SQS, RabbitMQ, or Kafka depending on existing infra)\n• Define NotificationRequested event schema\n• Migrate callers one at a time: Order first (highest volume), then Courier, Payment, Customer, Reporting\n• Keep synchronous path as fallback behind feature flag during migration\n\n**Wave 3 — Reporting decoupling (3-5 weeks):**\n• Set up CDC pipeline (Debezium) from Order DB to a dedicated Reporting read store\n• Build transformation layer that maps Order tables to Reporting's query model\n• Migrate Reporting queries to read store\n• Remove Reporting's direct DB access\n\n**Wave 4 — Database split & API decomposition (6-10 weeks):**\n• Introduce Order→Courier API for all cross-service reads\n• Dual-write during transition period with consistency checks\n• Split into separate PostgreSQL instances\n• Decompose Customer /everything into /profile, /addresses, /payment-methods, /orders, /preferences\n• Provide a BFF or composite endpoint for consumers that genuinely need multiple sub-resources",
    "files_to_create_or_modify": [
      "infrastructure/messaging/ — Message broker configuration and event schemas",
      "order-service/src/events/ — Domain event publishers (OrderPlaced, OrderStatusChanged)",
      "courier-service/src/api/ — New API endpoints for data that Order Service currently reads from shared DB",
      "notification-service/src/consumers/ — Event consumers replacing HTTP handlers",
      "customer-service/src/api/v2/ — Decomposed endpoints replacing /everything",
      "reporting-service/src/data/ — New data access layer pointing to read replica instead of Order DB",
      "infrastructure/cdc/ — Debezium/CDC configuration for Order→Reporting pipeline",
      "all-services/src/middleware/ — Circuit breaker and timeout configuration for Notification calls"
    ],
    "patterns_to_follow": "Strangler Fig for all migrations (never big-bang). Feature flags to control old-path vs new-path. Consumer-Driven Contract Tests between services. Schema Registry for event contracts. Circuit Breaker (Hystrix/Resilience4j pattern) as immediate stabilization.",
    "pitfalls_to_avoid": [
      "Do NOT attempt the database split and async migration simultaneously — sequence them",
      "Do NOT introduce an event bus without a Dead Letter Queue and monitoring from day one",
      "Do NOT decompose /everything without first cataloging every consumer and their actual field usage",
      "Do NOT remove the shared database access before verifying all cross-service queries have API equivalents",
      "Do NOT treat schema-prefix separation as a real boundary — it provides zero isolation guarantees",
      "Do NOT skip the dual-write validation phase during the database split — silent data drift is the #1 migration killer"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 85,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "All five anti-patterns are well-documented in distributed systems literature — the diagnosis is certain",
      "The remediation sequence (stabilize → async → decouple reads → split DB) is industry-standard",
      "Circuit breakers and auth on job endpoints are low-risk, high-value immediate wins"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact CDC tool choice depends on existing infrastructure (cloud provider, team expertise) which is unknown",
      "The Order-Courier database split complexity depends on the actual foreign key relationships and cross-schema joins — could be 6 weeks or 16 weeks",
      "Whether a full message broker or a simpler queue (SQS) is appropriate depends on throughput requirements not specified"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Services are independently deployable (separate processes/containers), not a modular monolith",
      "Team has or can acquire operational capability for a message broker",
      "There is no hard real-time requirement for notifications (async delivery with seconds of latency is acceptable)",
      "The Reporting Service's direct DB reads are SELECT-only, not writes",
      "Schema-prefix separation means something like 'order_*' and 'courier_*' table prefixes in the same database, not separate PostgreSQL schemas with permission boundaries"
    ]
  }
}
